heroHeadline: Supreme Court Weighs TPS for 350,000 Haitians; 2nd Circuit Unanimously Strikes Down Mandatory Detention; North Lake Hunger Strike Renewed
heroSubtitle: The Supreme Court heard oral arguments on April 29 in Mullin v. Doe and Trump v. Miot on whether the administration can revoke Temporary Protected Status for approximately 350,000 Haitians and 6,000 Syrians — with a ruling expected by June 2026 that could force mass displacement of families with U.S. citizen children who have lived in the U.S. for over a decade. In a separate unanimous 3-0 ruling, the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit struck down the Trump administration's mandatory detention (no-bond) policy, finding it misreads immigration law and raises serious constitutional due process questions, setting the stage for a likely Supreme Court confrontation. The ruling requires bond hearings for ICE detainees across New York, Connecticut, and Vermont. Meanwhile, detainees at the North Lake Processing Center in Michigan renewed a hunger strike citing unsafe conditions and medical neglect. These developments come against the backdrop of an intensifying judicial battle over the administration's immigration architecture — the 4th Circuit asylum ban ruling (April 24), BIA DACA precedent (April 25), El Gamal flight turnaround (April 26), and the House ending the 71-day DHS partial shutdown (April 26).
footerNote: Data sourced from DHS, ICE official records, ACLU, HRW, DHS OIG, TRAC Reports, Detention Watch Network, court records, and Congressional records
